title: Vegetable oil supply per person vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Average vegetable oil supply per capita, measured in kilograms per year versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Vegetable oil supply per person vs. GDP per capita, 2023

Average vegetable oil supply per capita, measured in kilograms per year versus gross domestic product (GDP) per capita, adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The scatter plot maps the relationship between national wealth and food availability, plotting GDP per capita on a logarithmic horizontal axis against vegetable oil supply in kilograms per year on the vertical axis. Data points are color-coded by continent, allowing for a visual comparison of regional distributions across the economic spectrum. A general upward trend is visible, where countries with higher GDP per capita tend to have higher vegetable oil supplies, though the correlation is not uniform. In the lower income range, specifically below $5,000 GDP per capita, supplies cluster between 5 and 10 kilograms; nations like the Democratic Republic of Congo, Niger, and Burkina Faso sit in this band. Moving toward the middle of the economic scale, distinct outliers emerge. Brazil and Algeria show supplies exceeding 20 kilograms despite having GDPs around $15,000 to $20,000, placing them well above the general trend line. Conversely, countries like Bolivia and Turkmenistan remain below 5 kilograms despite similar or higher income levels. In the high-income bracket, defined here as GDPs above $50,000, the supply levels vary significantly. Canada and the United States appear near the top of the chart with supplies approaching 30 kilograms, while Australia and Austria sit slightly lower in the 20 to 25 kilogram range. At the extreme right of the wealth axis, European nations such as Norway, Denmark, and Luxembourg show supplies that drop back down to the 5 to 10 kilogram range, demonstrating that the highest economic output does not strictly align with the highest vegetable oil consumption. The data shows a wide dispersion of values across all income levels, with no single continent monopolizing the highest or lowest positions.
